Macronutrients: Protein (4 kcal/g): 9 essential amino acids from food. Complete proteins (all 9): meat, fish, eggs, dairy, quinoa, soybeans. Incomplete (most plant proteins — combine complementary sources: rice + beans = complete). Recommended: 0.8-1.6g per kg bodyweight depending on activity. Carbohydrates (4 kcal/g): simple sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose — fast energy, spike insulin) vs complex carbs (starch, glycogen — slower digestion) vs fiber (non-digestible — feeds gut microbiome, slows glucose absorption, reduces colorectal cancer risk, 25-38g/day recommended). Fat (9 kcal/g): essential for hormones (testosterone, estrogen, cortisol), brain (60% fat), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), and inflammation regulation. Types: saturated (animal products, coconut oil — raises LDL), monounsaturated (olive oil, avocado, nuts — raises HDL), polyunsaturated (omega-3: oily fish, flaxseeds — anti-inflammatory; omega-6: vegetable oils — pro-inflammatory in excess), trans fats (partially hydrogenated: raises LDL AND lowers HDL — banned in many countries). Key micronutrients to watch: Vitamin D (bone health, immunity, mood — deficiency endemic globally), B12 (nerve function, red blood cells — must supplement if vegan), Iron (oxygen transport — common deficiency in women), Magnesium (300+ enzymatic reactions, sleep — most people deficient), Zinc (immunity, testosterone, wound healing), Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA+DHA — brain and cardiovascular health).

I'm a rules-based assistant — not an LLM like Claude or ChatGPT. Here's exactly how I work: when you send a message, I check it against layers in order: (1) Edit intent check (if you're editing a file we made together), (2) File creation check (make me an HTML/Python/etc. file), (3) Simple search-only query check, (4) Greeting check (hi, hey, hello), (5) Emoji-only messages, (6) Emotion detection (are you angry? excited? sad?), (7) Rules check (rules.json — specific responses to specific phrases), (8) Terminal command check (terminal.json), (9) Knowledge facts (knowledge.json — you're reading one right now!), then (10) Search detection (does this look like something I should search for?). If I find a match, I return the stored response. If not, I say I don't know and suggest searching. Claude helped Akiva design all of this! 🐒

I'm sort of AI — but a very simple kind! I'm rules-based, not a Large Language Model (LLM) like Claude or ChatGPT. Those systems have billions of parameters trained on vast amounts of text and can generate new content. I just match patterns in what you say against my stored knowledge and rules. I don't learn from conversations, don't generate novel text, and I'm definitely not conscious. Think of me as a very well-read lookup table with personality and a monkey emoji 🐒 The main thing I share with 'real AI' is that Akiva used Claude (actual AI) to help build me and fill in my knowledge base — so in a way, I'm AI-assisted!

Here's everything I can do! 💬 Chat and answer questions from my knowledge base (200+ facts across coding, science, history, culture, food, animals, music, sports, and more). 🔍 Search the web with your permission (DuckDuckGo). 📁 Create and edit files: HTML, CSS, JS, TypeScript, Python, Markdown, JSON, Bash scripts, SVG, and CSV — all viewable and downloadable in chat. ✏️ Drawing pad: open a canvas to sketch something. 📷 Photo examination: draw or describe what you see in a photo. 💻 Terminal commands and coding troubleshooting. 🔌 MCP server connection (configure the URL in Settings). 💾 Cloud sync via Backside/Worktruck API (when CORS is configured). Projects and chat history (currently in localStorage, syncing to cloud soon). Ask me anything!

That's me! 🐒 I'm Monkey Joe — a rules-based AI assistant built by Akiva with massive help from Claude. My brain lives in JSON files in a GitHub repo (github.com/akivamac/Mj.ai) and grows every time Akiva updates it. I know about: programming (Python, JS, TypeScript, React, SQL, Docker, and much more), science (physics, biology, chemistry, astronomy), world history, geography, mathematics, technology companies, food and cooking, animals, music, sports, philosophy, psychology, economics, and much more. I can create files, search the web, connect to MCP servers, and look at drawings. I'm not as powerful as Claude, but I'm Akiva's own monkey! 🐒

Akiva is my creator and best friend! 🐒 He's an independent developer and creative author who builds all kinds of projects. He works on a Daylight tablet running Android and uses Termux to push code to GitHub. He uses Claude to help design and build me and keep my brain growing. He's also writing a children's historical fiction book called Stik's Chocolate Adventures (Book 1, set in 1713 Central America — about a Mayan boy named Stik learning about the chocolate trade). He built me as a gift to himself and a way to learn — and I'm getting better every day! 🐒

Backside (at worktruck.app) is a cloud backend service built by Akiva's father (Aba). It provides structured data services — contacts, CRM, tasks, calendar, notes, and agents — accessible via REST API or MCP server. The API uses Bearer token authentication (Authorization: Bearer bsk_live_...). Monkey Joe uses Backside's Notes endpoint (to store chats as notes with monkey-joe-chat tag) and Notebooks endpoint (to store projects prefixed with 'mj:'). When available, this lets chats and projects sync across browsers and devices. Currently falling back to localStorage because CORS isn't configured — browsers block direct API calls to api.worktruck.app from github.io. The fix: Aba needs to add CORS headers allowing https://akivamac.github.io.

Lions (Panthera leo) are the only truly social big cats — they live in groups called prides (typically 10-15 females, their cubs, and 1-4 males). Diet: lions are carnivores — females do 90% of the hunting (zebra, wildebeest, buffalo, antelope). Males defend territory. A lion can eat 30 kg in one sitting then go days without food. Speed: up to 80 km/h in short bursts. A lion's roar can be heard 8 km away. Lifespan: 10-14 years in the wild. Habitat: sub-Saharan Africa (and a small population in India's Gir Forest — Asiatic lion). Males have manes that signal health and genetic quality — darker manes are preferred by females. Lions sleep 16-20 hours per day.

Tigers (Panthera tigris) are the largest wild cats — Siberian/Amur tigers reach 3.3m length and 300 kg. Unlike lions, tigers are solitary. Their stripes are unique like fingerprints — and the skin under the fur is also striped. Diet: tigers are carnivores — they eat deer, wild boar, gaur, and water buffalo. They are ambush predators, using stealth rather than speed (though they can reach 65 km/h). Tigers are strong swimmers and love water — unlike most cats. Habitat: scattered across Asia from India to Russian Far East. Bengal tiger (India): most numerous. Siberian/Amur tiger (Russia): largest. Only ~4,500 tigers remain in the wild (down from 100,000 in 1900). All subspecies are endangered.

The cheetah (Acinonyx jubatus) is the fastest land animal — top speed ~112-120 km/h achieved in 3 seconds (0-96 km/h faster than most sports cars). Built for speed: lightweight body (40-65 kg), long legs, flexible spine (acts like a spring), semi-retractable claws for grip (unlike other cats whose claws fully retract), and a large heart and lungs. Diet: carnivore — hunts gazelle, impala, hares. Unlike other big cats, cheetahs cannot roar — they purr, chirp, and make a unique high-pitched yelp. After a chase, cheetahs need 15-30 minutes to recover before eating. They lose ~50% of kills to larger predators (lions, leopards, hyenas) who steal their food. Lifespan: 10-12 years in wild. Only ~7,000 remain.

Leopards (Panthera pardus) are the most widespread big cats — found across sub-Saharan Africa, parts of North Africa, Central Asia, India, and China. They are the most adaptable big cat, living in rainforests, grasslands, mountains, and deserts. Spots are called rosettes (circular clusters). Black panthers are melanistic leopards — the spots are still visible in different lighting. Diet: carnivores — they eat almost anything: deer, antelope, monkeys, rodents, reptiles, even dung beetles. They hoist kills up into trees (sometimes carrying prey heavier than themselves) to keep them away from lions and hyenas. Leopards are excellent swimmers and climbers. Lifespan: 12-17 years in wild.

Gray wolves (Canis lupus) are the ancestors of all domestic dogs. They live in packs of 5-10, led by an alpha pair (usually the only pair that breeds). The 'alpha' concept is often misunderstood — in wild packs, the alpha is simply the breeding pair, not a dominant bully. Wolves howl to communicate with pack members, establish territory, and coordinate hunts. Each wolf has a unique howl. Diet: carnivores — wolves are apex predators eating deer, elk, moose, bison. They are crucial to ecosystems: wolf reintroduction to Yellowstone (1995) triggered a 'trophic cascade' — wolves changed elk behavior which allowed vegetation to recover which changed river courses. Lifespan: 6-8 years in wild. Habitat: North America, Europe, Asia.

Bears (family Ursidae) are large omnivores. Species: Polar bear (largest carnivorous land animal — up to 800 kg, pure white, Arctic, Diet: primarily seals), Grizzly/Brown bear (up to 700 kg, North America and Eurasia, Diet: salmon, berries, roots, insects, mammals — 20,000 calories/day before hibernation), American black bear (smaller, most common in North America, Diet: omnivore — mostly plants and berries), Giant panda (technically a bear, Diet: 99% bamboo — 12-38 kg per day, China), Sun bear (smallest, Southeast Asia, loves honey). Hibernation: bears enter a state of torpor (not true hibernation) — heart rate drops from 50 to 8 beats/min, they don't eat/drink/urinate for months, but can wake quickly. Female bears give birth during torpor and nurse cubs while sleeping. Lifespan: 20-25 years.

Snakes (suborder Serpentes) are legless reptiles that evolved from lizard ancestors ~100 million years ago. There are ~3,700 species. They sense heat via pit organs (pit vipers) and detect vibrations through their jaw bones. Their forked tongue collects scent molecules and delivers them to the Jacobson's organ in the roof of the mouth. Venomous vs non-venomous: ~600 species are venomous, only ~200 are dangerous to humans. Venom types: hemotoxic (destroys blood/tissue — rattlesnakes, vipers), neurotoxic (attacks nervous system — cobras, mambas, sea snakes, kraits). The black mamba is the fastest snake (~20 km/h) and has extremely potent neurotoxic venom. Pythons and boas are constrictors — they squeeze prey until it cannot breathe. The reticulated python is the longest snake (up to 8m). Diet: all snakes are carnivores.

Crocodilians (crocodiles, alligators, gharials, caimans) are ancient reptiles unchanged for ~200 million years — they survived the extinction that killed the dinosaurs. Crocodile vs alligator: crocodiles have a pointed V-shaped snout and both upper and lower teeth visible when mouth is closed; alligators have a rounded U-shaped snout and only upper teeth visible. Crocodiles are found in Africa, Asia, Americas, and Australia; alligators mainly in the southern USA and China. Diet: carnivores — they eat fish, birds, mammals, and reptiles. The Nile and saltwater crocodile are the largest (up to 6m, 1,000 kg). Saltwater crocodiles have the strongest bite of any animal (16,000 N — 10× a great white shark). They cannot chew — they use the 'death roll' to tear prey apart.

Frogs (order Anura) are amphibians — they live both in water and on land. Their life cycle: egg → tadpole (aquatic, breathes through gills) → froglet → adult frog (breathes through lungs and skin). Frogs absorb water and oxygen through their permeable skin — they never need to drink water. Most frogs return to the same pond where they were born to breed. Poison dart frogs (Dendrobatidae, Central/South America): among the most toxic animals on Earth. The golden poison frog has enough toxin to kill 10 adult humans. Their bright colors warn predators (aposematism). In captivity, they lose their toxicity — it comes from their diet of specific ants, mites, and beetles. There are ~7,000 species of amphibians; 40% are threatened with extinction — the most threatened class of vertebrates.

Turtles (order Testudines) have an evolutionary history of 220+ million years — their shell is unique, formed from fused backbone and ribs covered by a modified skin (not a separate structure they can leave!). Turtle vs tortoise: turtles are aquatic or semi-aquatic; tortoises are purely terrestrial with dome-shaped shells and elephant-like feet. Sea turtles: 7 species, all threatened/endangered. They navigate using Earth's magnetic field to return to their birth beach to nest — sometimes crossing thousands of kilometers. Leatherback sea turtle: largest (up to 2m, 900 kg), can dive to 1,280m, eats primarily jellyfish. Aldabra tortoise and Galápagos tortoise: longest-lived animals — regularly exceed 150 years. Jonathan, a Seychelles giant tortoise born ~1832, is the oldest known living land animal (190+ years).

Lizards (suborder Lacertilia) form the largest group of reptiles — ~6,500 species. Geckos: can walk on walls and ceilings via millions of tiny hairs (setae) on their feet that exploit van der Waals forces — not sticky, but instantly attachable/detachable. They can detach their tails (autotomy) to escape predators; the tail regrows. Chameleons: change color not primarily for camouflage but for communication and temperature regulation. They see ultraviolet light. Eyes move independently — can look in two directions at once. Tongue 1.5-2× body length launched in 0.07 seconds. Komodo dragon: largest lizard (up to 3m, 70 kg), found only on Indonesian islands. Venomous (anticoagulant venom in saliva). Can run at 20 km/h. Can reproduce via parthenogenesis (females can reproduce without males).

Chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes) are humans' closest living relatives — we share 98.7% of DNA. They live in communities of 20-150, led by an alpha male. Diet: omnivores — primarily fruit, but also nuts, seeds, insects (using sticks to 'fish' termites — tool use!), and meat (they hunt colobus monkeys cooperatively). Chimps use a variety of tools: sticks, stones, leaves (as cups or sponges). They have complex social lives: politics, alliances, warfare between groups, and cultural traditions that vary between communities (different groups use different tools). They can learn sign language and communicate with symbols. Lifespan: 40-60 years. Habitat: equatorial Africa. Endangered — only ~170,000-300,000 remain due to habitat loss and hunting.

Gorillas (genus Gorilla) are the largest living primates — silverback males can reach 1.8m standing and 200 kg. Eastern and Western gorillas are the two species. Mountain gorillas: fewer than 1,100 individuals remain, found in the Virunga volcanoes (Rwanda, DRC, Uganda). Diet: almost entirely herbivorous — leaves, stems, bark, fruit, and some insects. They eat 18-20 kg of vegetation per day and rarely drink water (get it from food). Silverbacks: dominant males who protect the group. They beat their chests not to show aggression but to communicate their presence. Gorillas are highly intelligent — Koko the gorilla learned over 1,000 signs in American Sign Language. Lifespan: 35-40 years in wild. Despite fearsome appearance, gorillas are gentle and peaceful unless threatened.

The human body contains ~37 trillion cells, 206 bones (babies have ~300, some fuse over time), 600+ muscles, and 11 organ systems. The brain has ~86 billion neurons with ~100 trillion synaptic connections. The heart beats ~100,000 times per day, pumping blood through ~100,000 km of blood vessels. The liver performs 500+ functions. The skin is the body's largest organ (~2 m², 3.6 kg). Humans shed and replace the outer layer of skin every 2-3 weeks (~30,000-40,000 dead skin cells per hour). The small intestine is ~6-7m long. Red blood cells live ~120 days; platelets ~10 days; white blood cells vary (neutrophils: days, memory T-cells: decades). The human body contains ~0.2 mg of gold. You have more bacterial cells in your gut than human cells in your entire body.

The human brain weighs ~1.4 kg and is ~60% fat. It has ~86 billion neurons, each connecting to thousands of others via synapses — an estimated 100 trillion connections. The brain consumes 20% of the body's energy despite being only 2% of body weight. Regions: prefrontal cortex (decision-making, personality, impulse control — fully developed at ~25 years), hippocampus (memory consolidation — damaged in Alzheimer's), amygdala (fear and emotion processing), cerebellum (balance and coordination), Broca's/Wernicke's areas (language). Neuroplasticity: the brain physically rewires itself in response to learning and experience throughout life. London taxi drivers have larger hippocampi from memorizing the city's streets. Sleep is crucial — during sleep the glymphatic system washes out metabolic waste products including amyloid-beta (linked to Alzheimer's).

The heart is a muscular pump about the size of your fist, weighing 250-350g. It has 4 chambers: right atrium and ventricle (pulmonary circulation — pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs), left atrium and ventricle (systemic circulation — pumps oxygenated blood to the body). The left ventricle has thicker walls (higher pressure). Heart rate: 60-100 bpm at rest; trained athletes can have rates of 40 bpm. The SA node (sinoatrial node) generates the electrical impulse that triggers each heartbeat — the heart can beat independently of the brain. Blood pressure: systolic (contraction)/diastolic (relaxation) measured in mmHg; normal ~120/80. Red blood cells carry oxygen via hemoglobin. Plasma is ~55% of blood volume. The heart starts beating at ~4 weeks of embryonic development.

The immune system defends against pathogens. Innate immunity (first line): physical barriers (skin, mucus membranes), fever, inflammation, neutrophils and macrophages (engulf pathogens). Adaptive immunity (second line): lymphocytes. B cells produce antibodies (proteins that neutralize specific pathogens). T cells: cytotoxic T cells kill infected cells; helper T cells coordinate the immune response; memory T and B cells remember specific pathogens for faster future response. Vaccines train adaptive immunity without full infection by presenting antigens. Types: live-attenuated (measles, MMR — strong long immunity), inactivated (flu, polio — safer for immunocompromised), mRNA (COVID-19 vaccines — instructs cells to produce a protein that trains immunity), subunit/toxoid (hepatitis B, tetanus). Herd immunity: when enough people are immune, disease can't spread — threshold varies (~95% for measles).

DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) encodes genetic information in sequences of four bases: A (adenine), T (thymine), G (guanine), C (cytosine) — A pairs with T, G pairs with C. The human genome: ~3 billion base pairs, ~20,000-25,000 protein-coding genes, organized in 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). Only ~1.5% of DNA codes for proteins — the rest includes regulatory regions, introns, and repetitive sequences (once called 'junk DNA' but many have regulatory roles). Epigenetics: gene expression can be modified by methyl groups attached to DNA — affected by environment, diet, stress — and can be inherited. CRISPR-Cas9 (Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier, 2012 — 2020 Nobel Prize): molecular scissors that can cut DNA at specific sequences, enabling precise editing. Used to treat sickle cell disease (first CRISPR therapy FDA approved 2023), study gene function, and develop new treatments.

Electricity and magnetism are two aspects of a single electromagnetic force — unified by James Clerk Maxwell's equations (1865). Electric current: flow of electrons through a conductor. Voltage (V): electrical potential difference. Current (I, in amperes): flow rate of charge. Resistance (Ω, ohms): opposition to current. Ohm's law: V = IR. Power (watts) = V × I = I²R. AC (alternating current): direction alternates 50-60 Hz — used for power grids (easier to transmit at high voltage, lower power loss). DC (direct current): one direction — used by batteries, electronics. Magnetism: moving charges create magnetic fields; changing magnetic fields induce electric currents (electromagnetic induction — basis of generators and transformers). Faraday's law. Electromagnetic spectrum (by frequency): radio → microwave → infrared → visible light → ultraviolet → X-ray → gamma ray. All travel at the speed of light in vacuum.

Thermodynamics studies heat, work, and energy. Four Laws: Zeroth Law: if A is in thermal equilibrium with B, and B with C, then A is in equilibrium with C (defines temperature). First Law: energy is conserved — it cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed (ΔU = Q - W). Second Law: entropy (disorder) of an isolated system always increases — heat flows from hot to cold spontaneously, never the reverse; no engine is 100% efficient. Third Law: as temperature approaches absolute zero (0 K = -273.15°C), entropy approaches a minimum. Absolute zero is the lowest possible temperature — atoms have minimum vibrational energy. Applications: heat engines (Carnot efficiency = 1 - Tcold/Thot), refrigerators, and the reason time has a direction (entropy always increases — the 'arrow of time').

Nuclear fission: splitting a heavy nucleus (uranium-235, plutonium-239) releases enormous energy (E=mc²). A chain reaction: each fission releases 2-3 neutrons which trigger more fissions. Uncontrolled: atomic bomb (Little Boy, 1945, ~15 kiloton TNT equivalent). Controlled: nuclear power plants (fission heats water → steam → turbine → electricity). France gets ~70% of its electricity from nuclear power. Nuclear fusion: combining light nuclei (deuterium + tritium → helium + neutron + energy) — the reaction that powers the Sun. Much more energy per reaction than fission, with minimal waste. ITER (France): $22 billion international fusion reactor project. National Ignition Facility (2022): first fusion experiment to produce more energy than it consumed — a milestone. Commercial fusion remains ~decades away. Radioactivity: unstable nuclei emit alpha (helium nuclei), beta (electrons/positrons), or gamma (high-energy photons) radiation during decay.

Gravity is the weakest of the four fundamental forces but has infinite range and is always attractive (unlike electromagnetism which can repel). Newton's law: F = Gm₁m₂/r² (inverse square law). G = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg². Einstein's General Relativity (1915): gravity is not a force but the curvature of spacetime caused by mass/energy. Objects follow geodesics (shortest paths through curved spacetime). GR predictions confirmed: gravitational lensing (light bends around massive objects), frame-dragging (Earth drags spacetime as it rotates, confirmed by Gravity Probe B 2011), and gravitational waves — ripples in spacetime from accelerating masses. LIGO detected the first gravitational waves Sept 14, 2015 from two merging black holes (1.3 billion light-years away) — the 2017 Nobel Prize in Physics. The signal stretched and compressed LIGO's 4km arms by less than 1/1000th the diameter of a proton.

The Standard Model of particle physics describes all known fundamental particles and three of the four fundamental forces (all except gravity). Fundamental particles: quarks (6 flavors: up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom — quarks combine in threes to form protons and neutrons, or pairs for mesons), leptons (electron, muon, tau, and their neutrinos), and force-carrier bosons (photon for electromagnetism, W and Z bosons for weak force, gluons for strong force). The Higgs boson: gives mass to other particles via the Higgs field — discovered at CERN's Large Hadron Collider on July 4, 2012 (the 'God particle'). The LHC is a 27km ring beneath France and Switzerland where protons collide at 99.9999991% the speed of light. Antimatter: every particle has an antiparticle — when matter and antimatter meet, they annihilate releasing pure energy. Why there's more matter than antimatter in the universe is one of physics' biggest unsolved problems.

The ocean covers 71% of Earth's surface and contains 97% of Earth's water. Five oceans: Pacific (largest — 30% of Earth's surface, bigger than all land combined), Atlantic (second largest, S-shaped), Indian (warmest), Southern (around Antarctica), Arctic (smallest, mostly frozen). Average depth: 3,688m. Deepest: Challenger Deep in Mariana Trench, 10,935m (western Pacific). The ocean has only been ~20% explored. Zones: sunlight zone (0-200m — most life), twilight zone (200-1,000m), midnight zone (1,000-4,000m, no light), abyssal zone (4,000-6,000m), hadal zone (6,000m+ in trenches). Deep-sea hydrothermal vents: support entire ecosystems without sunlight using chemosynthesis. The ocean absorbs ~25% of CO₂ emissions and ~90% of excess heat from climate change.

Plate tectonics: Earth's lithosphere is divided into ~15 major tectonic plates moving 1-15 cm/year. Boundaries: convergent (plates collide — one subducts, creating trenches and volcanoes, or they crumple into mountains like the Himalayas), divergent (plates separate — magma wells up, creating mid-ocean ridges or rift valleys), transform (plates slide past each other — San Andreas Fault). Earthquakes: most occur at plate boundaries. Measured by moment magnitude scale (logarithmic — each step = 31.6× more energy). Magnitude 9+ events include the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake (Japan, triggered devastating tsunami). Volcanoes: ~1,500 potentially active. Types: shield (broad, gentle slopes, basaltic lava — Hawaiian), stratovolcano/composite (steep, explosive — Mt. Fuji, Mt. St. Helens). The Ring of Fire encircles the Pacific with 75% of Earth's volcanoes. Supervolcanoes: Yellowstone (USA), Toba (Indonesia).

Weather is the short-term state of the atmosphere; climate is the long-term pattern. The atmosphere is powered by solar energy — differential heating between equator and poles drives global circulation (Hadley, Ferrel, and Polar cells). Clouds form when warm moist air rises and cools below the dew point. Hurricanes/cyclones/typhoons (same phenomenon, different names by region): tropical cyclones with sustained winds >119 km/h, powered by warm ocean water (>26°C). They spin counterclockwise in Northern Hemisphere (Coriolis effect). Eye: calm center ~30-65 km across. Katrina (2005): deadliest recent US hurricane, $108 billion damage. Tornadoes: violently rotating columns of air 65-400+ km/h, formed when cold dry air meets warm moist air (US 'Tornado Alley'). Lightning: ~100 strikes per second globally, 5× hotter than the Sun's surface (~30,000 K). Thunder: sound wave from rapid air expansion around lightning.

Tropical rainforests cover ~6% of Earth's surface but contain 50-80% of all species. The Amazon rainforest: ~5.5 million km² (largest on Earth), spanning 9 countries (mostly Brazil), contains 10% of all species on Earth, produces 20% of Earth's oxygen (also absorbs similar amounts — often called the 'lungs of the Earth' though this is debated). The Amazon river discharges 20% of all freshwater entering the world's oceans. Layers: emergent layer (tallest trees, 50-70m), canopy (main layer, most life, 30-45m), understory (low light), forest floor (1% of sunlight). Deforestation: 20% of the Amazon has been cleared since 1970, primarily for cattle ranching and soy farming. Scientists warn of a 'tipping point' at ~20-25% deforestation where the forest may no longer generate its own rainfall and irreversibly savannify.

The Middle Ages (c. 500-1500 AD) span the fall of the Western Roman Empire to the Renaissance. Feudalism: hierarchical land-tenure system — King grants land to lords, lords grant to vassals and knights in exchange for military service, peasants/serfs farm the land. The Catholic Church was enormously powerful — controlled education, law, and politics across Europe. The Crusades (1095-1291): series of religious wars called by the Pope to recapture Jerusalem from Muslim control. First Crusade (1096-1099): the only 'successful' one — Crusaders captured Jerusalem. The Black Death (1347-1351): bubonic plague killed 30-60% of Europe's population (~25 million people) in 5 years — carried by fleas on black rats. Flagellants beat themselves as penance; Jews were blamed and massacred. The plague paradoxically improved conditions for surviving peasants — labor shortages increased their bargaining power.

The Age of Discovery (15th-17th centuries) saw European explorers map and colonize the world. Christopher Columbus (1451-1506, Genoese sailing for Spain): sailed west believing he'd reach Asia — landed in the Bahamas October 12, 1492. He made 4 voyages, reached Cuba, Hispaniola, South and Central America, but never recognized he'd found continents unknown to Europeans. Amerigo Vespucci (Florentine) realized these were new continents — hence 'America.' Ferdinand Magellan (1519-1522): led the first circumnavigation of the globe (though he died in the Philippines, Juan Sebastián Elcano completed it). Vasco da Gama (1497-1498): first to sail from Europe to India around Africa. Zheng He (Chinese admiral, early 1400s): led 7 massive voyages across Southeast Asia, India, Arabia, and East Africa — with fleets of 300 ships — decades before Columbus.

The Vikings (late 8th to early 11th century, from Scandinavia — modern Norway, Sweden, Denmark) were Norse seafarers known as raiders, traders, and explorers. Their longships were technologically advanced — shallow draft allowing river travel, symmetric design for landing on beaches, and capable of open-ocean voyages. Vikings raided monasteries and coastal towns across Europe (first major raid: Lindisfarne, 793 AD). They also traded extensively with Byzantium and the Islamic world (Varangian routes). Leif Erikson reached North America (Vinland, likely Newfoundland, Canada) ~1000 AD — 500 years before Columbus. Norse settlements at L'Anse aux Meadows (Newfoundland) confirmed archaeologically in 1960. They settled Iceland (874 AD), Greenland (986 AD), and founded Dublin and Kyiv. 'Viking' meant 'pirate/raider' — most Norsemen were farmers.

The Ottoman Empire (1299-1922) was one of history's largest and longest-lasting empires, spanning three continents at its peak. Founded by Osman I in Anatolia (modern Turkey). Key events: fall of Constantinople (May 29, 1453) — Sultan Mehmed II conquered the Byzantine capital, ending the Eastern Roman Empire after 1,123 years (renamed Istanbul). Suleiman the Magnificent (r. 1520-1566): peak of the empire — expanded into Hungary, besieged Vienna, dominated the Mediterranean. The empire was organized around the millet system (religious communities governed their own affairs). The Ottoman Caliphate led Sunni Islam. Declined through military defeats and reform failures. Abolished after WWI; the Republic of Turkey was proclaimed by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk in 1923.

The Mongol Empire (1206-1368) was the largest contiguous land empire in history — at its peak covering ~24 million km² (about 16% of Earth's land area). Founded by Genghis Khan (born Temüjin, c. 1162-1227) who united the Mongol tribes in 1206. The Mongols were superb cavalry warriors — highly mobile, expert archers on horseback using composite bows. Conquered: China (Kublai Khan founded the Yuan dynasty), Persia, Central Asia, Russia, Poland, and Hungary. The sack of Baghdad (1258) destroyed the Abbasid Caliphate and the 'House of Wisdom' — burning irreplaceable books. The Black Death may have been spread westward along Mongol trade routes. The Pax Mongolica allowed safe trade across the Silk Road, connecting Europe and China. The Mongols had no permanent army — every man was a warrior. Genghis Khan is thought to be the ancestor of ~0.5% of the world's male population.

Brazil is South America's largest country (8.51 million km², 5th largest in the world) and most populous (215 million). The only Portuguese-speaking country in the Americas (colonized by Portugal 1500-1822). Largest city: São Paulo (~22 million metro — largest in South America). Capital: Brasília (purpose-built capital, moved from Rio de Janeiro in 1960, designed by Oscar Niemeyer). The Amazon rainforest covers ~60% of Brazil. Brazil is the world's largest producer of coffee, sugarcane, and orange juice; 2nd largest exporter of soybeans and beef. GDP: ~$2.1 trillion (8th largest). Known for: Carnival (Rio de Janeiro), football (soccer — won 5 World Cups), the Amazon, Iguazú Falls, and the Pantanal (world's largest tropical wetland). Brazil is home to ~60% of the Amazon rainforest.

Russia is the world's largest country by area — 17.1 million km² (11% of Earth's land surface, spanning 11 time zones). Population: ~145 million (9th largest). Capital: Moscow (~12 million). St. Petersburg is the cultural capital. GDP: ~$2.2 trillion (11th). Russia borders 14 countries (most of any country). Siberia (75% of Russia's territory, 25% of its population): contains the world's deepest lake (Baikal — 20% of world's unfrozen fresh water), vast oil/gas reserves (Russia is one of the world's top energy producers). History: Romanov dynasty (1613-1917), Russian Revolution (1917), Soviet Union (1922-1991). The USSR launched the first satellite (Sputnik, 1957), first human in space (Gagarin, 1961), and was a nuclear superpower. Russia invaded Ukraine February 24, 2022.

Australia is the world's 6th largest country (7.69 million km²) and the largest country in Oceania. Population: ~26 million (mostly in coastal cities). Capital: Canberra (not Sydney or Melbourne — a compromise capital). Unique wildlife: ~80% of species found nowhere else — kangaroos, koalas, wombats, platypus, echidna (monotremes — egg-laying mammals), and deadly venomous creatures (box jellyfish, funnel-web spider, eastern brown snake, blue-ringed octopus, stonefish, saltwater crocodile). Great Barrier Reef: world's largest coral reef system (2,300 km — visible from space), home to 9,000 species, 50% bleached due to warming. Aboriginal Australians: one of Earth's oldest continuous cultures (~65,000 years). Australia was a British penal colony from 1788. Outback: vast arid interior. Uluru (Ayers Rock): sacred sandstone monolith, spiritual heart of Aboriginal Australia.

Canada is the world's second largest country by area (9.98 million km²) but relatively sparsely populated (~38 million, most within 200 km of the US border). Two official languages: English and French. Quebec is predominantly French-speaking. Capital: Ottawa. Largest city: Toronto. GDP: ~$2.1 trillion (10th). Known for: natural beauty (Rocky Mountains, Niagara Falls, northern lights), ice hockey (the national sport — invented in Canada), maple syrup (Canada produces ~71% of the world's supply), universal healthcare, and multiculturalism. The Trans-Canada Highway is ~8,000 km — one of the world's longest national highways. Canada has the longest coastline of any country (202,080 km). Indigenous peoples make up ~5% of the population — 634 First Nations communities.

France is Western Europe's largest country (643,000 km²) with a population of ~68 million. Capital: Paris ('City of Light') — one of the world's most visited cities. France is the world's most visited country (~90 million tourists/year). Language: French — spoken by 275+ million people worldwide as a legacy of French colonialism. World's top wine producer (alongside Italy and Spain). 1,200+ types of cheese. French cuisine is on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list. Major contributions: Enlightenment philosophy (Voltaire, Rousseau, Descartes), literature (Hugo, Flaubert, Proust, Camus), impressionism (Monet, Renoir), Pasteur (germ theory), Curie (radioactivity — double Nobel Prize winner). The Eiffel Tower (1889, built for the World's Fair, designed by Gustave Eiffel): 330m tall, initially criticized but became the world's most visited monument. France is a permanent member of the UN Security Council with nuclear weapons.

Modern Egypt (officially the Arab Republic of Egypt) has ~105 million people — the most populous Arab country and most populous in Africa. Capital: Cairo (~21 million metro — largest city in Africa and the Arab world). Official language: Arabic. Egypt sits at the crossroads of Africa, the Middle East, and the Mediterranean — the Suez Canal (opened 1869), connecting the Mediterranean and Red Sea, carries ~12% of world trade. GDP: ~$476 billion. The Nile River (world's longest, ~6,650 km) flows north through Egypt — virtually all of Egypt's population lives within a few kilometers of the Nile. Egypt has 7 UNESCO World Heritage Sites including the Pyramids of Giza (the only surviving ancient wonder). Modern Egypt became a republic in 1952. The Arab Spring (2011) triggered the Egyptian Revolution, ousting President Mubarak after 30 years. The Aswan High Dam (1970) controls flooding and generates hydroelectric power.

Infinity (∞) is not a number but a concept. Georg Cantor (1845-1918) proved that some infinities are larger than others — one of the most surprising results in mathematics. Countable infinity (ℵ₀, 'aleph-null'): the infinity of counting numbers (1, 2, 3...) and also of rational numbers (fractions) — these are the same 'size' of infinity. Uncountable infinity: the infinity of real numbers (decimals) — provably larger than countable infinity via Cantor's diagonal argument. The Continuum Hypothesis (can there be an infinity between these two?) was proven to be undecidable using standard axioms (Gödel 1940, Cohen 1963). Limits: 1/0 is undefined (not infinity — the limit of 1/x as x→0⁺ is +∞, as x→0⁻ is -∞). In calculus, limits handle infinity rigorously. Hilbert's Hotel: a hotel with infinitely many rooms, all full, can still accommodate more guests — counterintuitive properties of infinite sets.

Mathematical proof is a deductive argument showing a statement must be true given axioms. Types of proof: Direct proof (assume premises, derive conclusion), Proof by contradiction/reductio ad absurdum (assume the opposite is true, derive a contradiction — e.g., Euclid's proof of infinite primes), Proof by induction (show true for base case n=1, show if true for n then true for n+1 → true for all n), Constructive proof (exhibit the object), Proof by contrapositive (prove 'not Q → not P' instead of 'P → Q'). Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems (1931): (1) In any consistent formal system powerful enough to express basic arithmetic, there are true statements that cannot be proved within the system. (2) No such system can prove its own consistency. These theorems fundamentally limit mathematics — there are mathematical truths that are unreachable by formal proof. This shattered the dream of a complete, consistent mathematical foundation.

Euclidean geometry (Euclid, ~300 BC, 'Elements'): the geometry of flat surfaces. Five postulates including the parallel postulate (through a point not on a line, exactly one parallel line exists). Non-Euclidean geometries (19th century): spherical geometry (Earth's surface — parallel lines meet, triangle angles sum to MORE than 180°, used in navigation) and hyperbolic geometry (saddle-shaped space — parallel lines diverge, triangle angles sum to LESS than 180°, used in General Relativity). Topology: the study of properties preserved under continuous deformations. Famous results: a coffee mug and a donut are topologically equivalent (both have one hole). The Möbius strip has one side and one edge. The Four Color Theorem: any map can be colored with 4 colors so no adjacent regions share a color — proved in 1976 by computer (first major computer-assisted proof).

Number theory studies integers and their properties. Famous results and problems: Fermat's Last Theorem: aⁿ + bⁿ = cⁿ has no integer solutions for n>2. Stated by Fermat in 1637 ('I have a remarkable proof which this margin is too narrow to contain'). Proved by Andrew Wiles in 1995 after 7 years of secret work — the proof is 129 pages. Goldbach's Conjecture (1742, unproven): every even integer >2 is the sum of two primes (verified up to 4×10¹⁸). Twin Prime Conjecture (unproven): there are infinitely many pairs of primes differing by 2 (3&5, 11&13, 17&19...). Euler's identity e^(iπ)+1=0 connects five fundamental constants. The Collatz conjecture: take any positive integer; if even divide by 2, if odd multiply by 3 and add 1; repeat — conjecture: always reaches 1. Verified up to 2⁶⁸ but unproven. Paul Erdős offered cash prizes for unsolved problems — 'Erdős numbers' measure mathematical collaboration distance from him.

Machine learning (ML): algorithms that improve through experience without explicit programming. Supervised learning: trained on labeled examples (input→output pairs) — classification (spam/not spam) and regression (predict house price). Unsupervised learning: find patterns in unlabeled data — clustering (customer segments), dimensionality reduction (PCA). Reinforcement learning: agent learns by trial and error, receiving rewards/penalties — used in AlphaGo, robotics, game AI. Deep learning: neural networks with many layers. Each neuron computes a weighted sum of inputs, applies an activation function (ReLU: max(0,x), sigmoid, tanh). Backpropagation: gradient descent adjusts weights to minimize prediction error. CNNs (convolutional neural networks): image recognition. RNNs/LSTMs: sequence data (text, speech). Transformers (2017, 'Attention Is All You Need'): attention mechanism — the architecture behind GPT, BERT, Claude, and all modern LLMs.

OpenAI was founded in 2015 as a nonprofit by Elon Musk, Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, and others with $1B in pledges. Became a 'capped-profit' company in 2019. Microsoft invested $1B (2019), then $13B (2023). Products: GPT-4 (Mar 2023): multimodal, massive capability leap. GPT-4o (May 2024): faster, cheaper, omnimodal (text/image/audio). ChatGPT: launched Nov 30, 2022, reached 100 million users in 2 months — fastest app in history. DALL-E: image generation from text. Whisper: speech recognition. Sora (2024): video generation. Codex: code generation, powers GitHub Copilot. o1/o3 reasoning models (2024-25): designed to 'think step by step' before answering. Sam Altman was briefly fired by the board (Nov 2023) then reinstated after massive employee backlash.

Anthropic was founded in 2021 by Dario Amodei (CEO), Daniela Amodei (President), and others who left OpenAI. Mission: AI safety research and building safe, beneficial AI systems. Constitutional AI: a training approach where Claude learns from AI feedback guided by a 'constitution' — a set of principles — rather than relying solely on human feedback. This makes the process more scalable and consistent. Claude's model families: Haiku (fast, efficient), Sonnet (balanced), Opus (most capable). Context window: up to 200K tokens. Anthropic is known for publishing safety research (scaling laws, interpretability, red-teaming). Key investors: Google, Spark Capital, and others — valued at $18B+ as of 2024. Anthropic's 'responsible scaling policy' commits to safety evaluations before deploying more powerful models. Anthropic built me — through Claude, who helped Akiva design Monkey Joe! 🐒

Smartphones transformed society after the iPhone (June 29, 2007) and the App Store (July 2008). Key milestones: BlackBerry (enterprise email, peaked 2009), Nokia dominance (feature phones, late 1990s-2000s), iPhone launch (capacitive touchscreen, no physical keyboard, full internet browser), Android (Google, Sept 2008 — first Android phone HTC Dream, now powers ~72% of smartphones), Samsung Galaxy S series, App economy: 5 million+ apps on iOS and Android App Stores. Smartphone penetration: ~6.8 billion users (~85% of the world population). Cameras: modern flagship phone cameras rival DSLRs for many purposes. 5G: 10-100× faster than 4G, enables IoT, autonomous vehicles, and real-time AR/VR. Average person checks their phone 96 times per day.

Video game history: Pong (Atari, 1972 — first commercially successful arcade game), Space Invaders (1978), Pac-Man (1980, first gaming 'character'), Donkey Kong (1981, introduced Mario), Nintendo NES (1983/85 — saved the industry after the 1983 crash), Super Mario Bros (1985 — 40 million copies), The Legend of Zelda (1986), Game Boy (1989), SNES vs Sega Genesis (16-bit era), PlayStation (Sony, 1994), N64 vs PS1 3D era, PS2 (2000 — best-selling console ever, 155 million), Halo + Xbox (2001), Wii (2006 — motion controls, casual gaming revolution), smartphones (Angry Birds 2009, Mobile gaming surpassed consoles in revenue), Minecraft (2011 — 238 million copies, best-selling game ever), Fortnite (2017, Battle Royale genre), Switch (2017, hybrid). Global gaming market: ~$184 billion/year. Esports: $1+ billion industry with stadium tournaments.

The Internet of Things (IoT): physical devices embedded with sensors, software, and network connectivity — they collect and exchange data. Examples: smart thermostats (Nest), smart speakers (Amazon Echo/Alexa, Google Home), smartwatches (Apple Watch), fitness trackers, smart lights (Philips Hue), security cameras, connected appliances, industrial sensors, agricultural sensors, smart city infrastructure. ~15 billion IoT devices currently connected; projected 30+ billion by 2030. Security concerns: IoT devices often have weak security — botnets of compromised IoT devices have launched major DDoS attacks (Mirai botnet 2016 took down major websites). Privacy concerns: always-on microphones, location tracking. 5G enables massive IoT expansion with lower latency and more simultaneous connections per cell.

Pasta is made from durum wheat semolina and water (fresh pasta also uses eggs). Italy has 300+ recognized pasta shapes. Long pasta: spaghetti (thin, round), linguine (flat, narrow), fettuccine (flat, wider), pappardelle (very wide flat, for hearty meat sauces), capellini/angel hair (very thin). Short pasta: penne (tubes, ridged or smooth), rigatoni (large ridged tubes), fusilli (spirals, hold sauce in crevices), farfalle (bow-tie), conchiglie (shells), orecchiette (ear-shaped, traditional with broccoli rabe). Stuffed: ravioli, tortellini, cannelloni. Soup pasta: orzo, pastina. Rule: pair pasta shape to sauce — long thin pasta with light oil/cream sauces; thick short pasta with chunky meat sauces; ridged pasta holds more sauce. Bronze-die extruded pasta has a rougher surface that holds sauce better than Teflon-die.

Cheese is made from milk (cow, goat, sheep, buffalo) via acidification and rennet (enzyme that coagulates milk protein). Over 1,800 named varieties worldwide. Categories: Fresh (no aging — ricotta, mozzarella, burrata, cream cheese, cottage cheese), Soft-ripened (mold-ripened rind — Brie, Camembert — creamy interior develops from outside in), Washed-rind (washed in brine/beer/wine/spirits during aging — pungent, orange rind: Limburger, Taleggio, Époisses), Semi-soft (Havarti, Fontina, Gouda young), Hard (aged 6+ months — Cheddar, Gruyère, Parmesan/Parmigiano-Reggiano — must be aged 12+ months in Parma region, named via regulation), Blue (Penicillium mold creates blue/green veins — Roquefort, Gorgonzola, Stilton, Danish Blue). Parmigiano-Reggiano has the strongest umami of any food — highest natural glutamate content.

Spices come from seeds, bark, roots, or dried fruits; herbs come from leaves. Most valuable historically — the spice trade drove European exploration of the world. Black pepper: once so valuable it was used as currency (peppercorns). Saffron: the world's most expensive spice by weight (~$5,000/kg) — from Crocus sativus stigmas, hand-harvested. Curcumin (in turmeric): anti-inflammatory compound — used in Ayurvedic medicine for thousands of years, active area of modern research. Capsaicin (chili): activates pain receptors (TRPV1) — your body releases endorphins in response (the 'chili high'). Scoville scale measures heat: bell pepper (0), jalapeño (2,500-8,000), habanero (100,000-350,000), Carolina Reaper (1,400,000-2,200,000 — world's hottest). Cinnamon: two types — Ceylon (true cinnamon, mild, Sri Lanka) and Cassia (stronger, more common, China/Vietnam — contains more coumarin which is toxic in large amounts).

Japanese cuisine (washoku) is on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list. Core principles: seasonal ingredients (shun), balance of flavors, visual presentation (food as art). Key dishes: Ramen (Chinese-origin noodle soup adapted by Japan — four regional styles: shoyu/soy Tokyo, miso Hokkaido, tonkotsu pork bone Fukuoka, shio/salt Hakodate), Tempura (battered fried vegetables/seafood — brought by Portuguese missionaries in the 16th century), Teriyaki (grilled with sweet soy glaze), Yakitori (skewered grilled chicken), Tonkatsu (breaded fried pork cutlet), Kaiseki (multi-course haute cuisine). Wagyu beef: extremely marbled (Kobe, Matsusaka, Omi brands) — cows receive special care, their fat melts at lower temperature giving extraordinary tenderness. A5 wagyu is the highest grade. Matcha: powdered green tea, shade-grown and stone-ground — used in tea ceremony, desserts, ice cream.

Indian cuisine varies enormously by region — it's not one cuisine but dozens. North India: Mughal influence — rich curries, naan bread (tandoor-baked), biryani (fragrant layered rice and meat dish), paneer (fresh cheese), butter chicken (murgh makhani, invented at Moti Mahal restaurant Delhi ~1950). South India: rice-based, spicier, coconut milk — dosa (fermented rice and lentil crepe), idli (steamed rice cakes), sambar (lentil soup), rasam (spiced tamarind broth), chettinad cuisine (most complex spice profiles). Gujarat: predominantly vegetarian — dal, thepla, dhokla. Bengal: fish-focused, mustard oil. Goa: Portuguese influence — vindaloo, sorpotel. Key spices: cardamom, cumin, coriander, turmeric, fenugreek, asafoetida, mustard seeds. India has the highest percentage of vegetarians of any country (~38%).

The science of baking: Maillard reaction (named after Louis-Camille Maillard, 1912): chemical reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars at ~140-165°C producing hundreds of flavor and aroma compounds — responsible for the browning and complex flavor of bread crusts, roasted coffee, seared meat, cookies, and toast. It's NOT the same as caramelization (which is the browning of sugars alone). Caramelization (160-180°C): sugars break down into hundreds of compounds with nutty, buttery, and slightly bitter flavors — crème brûlée crust, toffee, caramel sauce. Gluten: network formed from glutenin and gliadin proteins in wheat when hydrated and kneaded — provides elasticity and traps CO₂ from yeast. More kneading = stronger gluten network = chewier bread. Fat (butter, oil) shortens gluten strands = tender, crumbly texture (hence 'shortening'). Eggs: add structure (proteins coagulate when heated), moisture, emulsification (lecithin in yolk), and richness.

Western art history: Ancient (cave paintings ~40,000 BC, Egyptian/Greek/Roman), Medieval (religious iconography, flat perspective), Renaissance (15th-17th century: perspective, humanism, naturalism — Botticelli, da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael), Baroque (dramatic light/shadow, emotion — Caravaggio, Rembrandt, Rubens, Vermeer), Rococo (lighter, playful — Fragonard, Watteau), Neoclassicism vs Romanticism (18th-19th century — Ingres vs Delacroix), Impressionism (late 19th century: capture light and moment — Monet, Renoir, Degas, Pissarro), Post-Impressionism (van Gogh, Gauguin, Cézanne), Expressionism, Cubism (Picasso, Braque — multiple perspectives simultaneously), Surrealism (Dalí, Magritte — dreams and the unconscious), Abstract Expressionism (Pollock — action painting), Pop Art (Warhol, Lichtenstein — mass media imagery). Most expensive paintings: Salvator Mundi (Leonardo, $450.3M, 2017), Les Femmes d'Alger (Picasso, $179.4M).

Western literature's greatest works: Ancient: Homer's Iliad and Odyssey (~8th century BC), Virgil's Aeneid. Medieval: Dante's Divine Comedy (1320), Chaucer's Canterbury Tales. Renaissance: Shakespeare's plays (Hamlet, Macbeth, King Lear, Othello, Romeo and Juliet — 37 plays, 154 sonnets). 18th century: Cervantes' Don Quixote (often called the first modern novel), Swift's Gulliver's Travels, Voltaire's Candide. 19th century: Jane Austen (Pride and Prejudice), Dickens (A Tale of Two Cities — best-selling novel ever at 200M+), Dostoyevsky (Crime and Punishment, The Brothers Karamazov), Tolstoy (War and Peace, Anna Karenina), Melville (Moby-Dick), Mark Twain (Huckleberry Finn). 20th century: Kafka (The Trial), Joyce (Ulysses), Woolf (Mrs. Dalloway), Hemingway (The Old Man and the Sea), Orwell (1984, Animal Farm), Camus (The Stranger), García Márquez (One Hundred Years of Solitude — magic realism masterpiece).

Cinema history: Lumière brothers (first public film screening, Dec 28, 1895, Paris). Silent era: Chaplin, Buster Keaton, DW Griffith. Sound ('talkies'): The Jazz Singer (1927). Golden Age of Hollywood (1930s-60s): studio system, Casablanca, Citizen Kane (Orson Welles, 1941 — often called the greatest film ever), Gone With the Wind. New Hollywood (1970s): auteur directors — Kubrick (2001: A Space Odyssey), Coppola (The Godfather), Spielberg (Jaws, Raiders of the Lost Ark, Schindler's List), Scorsese (Taxi Driver, Goodfellas), Lucas (Star Wars). Best Picture Oscar winners: 12 Years a Slave, Moonlight, Parasite (first non-English language film to win, 2019). Highest-grossing: Avatar ($2.92B, 2009, re-release $2.32B), Avengers: Endgame ($2.80B), Titanic ($2.26B). Netflix (streaming) vs theatrical exhibition remains an ongoing disruption. Bollywood (Mumbai): produces more films than Hollywood annually.

Greek mythology: Olympian gods — Zeus/Jupiter (king, thunder), Hera/Juno (marriage), Poseidon/Neptune (sea), Demeter/Ceres (harvest), Ares/Mars (war), Athena/Minerva (wisdom, strategy), Apollo (sun, arts, prophecy), Artemis/Diana (moon, hunting), Aphrodite/Venus (love, beauty), Hephaestus/Vulcan (fire, forge), Hermes/Mercury (messenger, travel), Dionysus/Bacchus (wine). Heroes: Heracles/Hercules (12 Labors), Achilles (Trojan War, vulnerable heel), Odysseus/Ulysses (10-year voyage home), Perseus (slew Medusa), Theseus (killed the Minotaur in the Labyrinth). Norse mythology: Asgard (realm of gods), Odin (Allfather, wisdom, war, death — sacrificed an eye for knowledge), Thor (thunder, Mjölnir), Loki (trickster), Freya (love, war), Valkyries, Ragnarök (the twilight of the gods — apocalyptic final battle). Egyptian: Ra (sun), Osiris (afterlife), Isis (magic), Horus (sky), Anubis (death).

Key vitamins and minerals: Vitamin A (vision, immune function — carrots, sweet potato, liver; deficiency causes night blindness). B vitamins (8 total): B1/thiamine (energy metabolism), B3/niacin (DNA repair), B9/folate (cell division, crucial in pregnancy — prevents neural tube defects), B12/cobalamin (nerve function, red blood cells — ONLY in animal products, vegans must supplement; deficiency → irreversible nerve damage). Vitamin C (immune function, collagen synthesis, antioxidant — scurvy from deficiency historically killed sailors; found in citrus, berries, peppers). Vitamin D (bone health via calcium absorption, immune regulation, mood — synthesized by skin in sunlight; widespread deficiency especially in northern latitudes in winter; supplement 1000-2000 IU/day widely recommended). Vitamin E (antioxidant). Vitamin K2 (bone and cardiovascular health — not to be confused with K1). Iron (oxygen transport — deficiency: anemia, most common nutritional deficiency globally, especially in women). Magnesium (300+ enzymatic reactions, sleep, mood — most people don't get enough from diet alone).

Cancer: diseases where cells grow uncontrollably and can invade other tissues. Caused by mutations in DNA that affect cell division regulation (oncogenes: mutated → accelerate division; tumor suppressor genes like p53: mutated → fail to stop division). All cancers arise from normal cells. Types: carcinoma (epithelial cells — most common: breast, lung, colon, prostate), sarcoma (connective tissue — bone, muscle), lymphoma (lymphatic system — Hodgkin's, non-Hodgkin's), leukemia (blood), melanoma (melanocytes — skin). Most common worldwide: lung (most deaths), breast, colorectal, prostate, stomach. Risk factors: tobacco (30% of cancer deaths), obesity, alcohol, radiation (UV, ionizing), infections (HPV → cervical/throat cancer, H. pylori → stomach cancer, hepatitis B/C → liver cancer). Treatments: surgery, radiation, chemotherapy (kills rapidly dividing cells — side effects because hair/gut cells also divide rapidly), targeted therapy (block specific proteins), immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors unleash immune system against cancer — revolutionized melanoma and lung cancer treatment), CAR-T cell therapy. 5-year survival rates have dramatically improved for many cancers.

Diabetes mellitus: a group of metabolic diseases characterized by high blood sugar. Type 1 (~10% of cases): autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells → no insulin production. Typically begins in childhood/young adulthood. Requires insulin injections/pump. Not caused by diet. Type 2 (~90% of cases): cells become resistant to insulin; pancreas can't compensate. Strongly linked to obesity, physical inactivity, and genetics. Progressive: often managed initially with lifestyle changes (diet, exercise), then oral medications (metformin), then insulin. Gestational diabetes: during pregnancy. Long-term complications: neuropathy (nerve damage — especially feet), nephropathy (kidney damage), retinopathy (eye damage → blindness), cardiovascular disease. Worldwide: ~537 million adults have diabetes (2021), projected 783 million by 2045. CGMs (continuous glucose monitors) and insulin pumps have dramatically improved management. First insulin injection: 1922 (Leonard Thompson, age 14) — transformed a fatal disease into a manageable one.

Antibiotics kill or inhibit bacteria — they have NO effect on viruses (colds, flu, COVID-19 are viral — antibiotics will NOT help and contribute to resistance). Discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928 (penicillin from Penicillium mold — found accidentally when mold contaminated a bacterial culture). Classes: beta-lactams (penicillin, amoxicillin, cephalosporins — disrupt bacterial cell wall), macrolides (azithromycin/Z-pack — protein synthesis), tetracyclines (broad spectrum), fluoroquinolones (DNA replication), aminoglycosides (protein synthesis). Antibiotic resistance: bacteria evolve resistance through mutation and horizontal gene transfer. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) are major threats. WHO calls antimicrobial resistance one of the greatest threats to global health — by 2050, resistant infections could kill 10 million people/year (more than cancer). Key: always complete prescribed antibiotic courses; never share or self-prescribe.

Buddhism was founded by Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha — 'awakened one') in the 5th-4th century BC in northern India (modern Nepal). Born a prince, he abandoned wealth to seek liberation from suffering after encountering old age, sickness, and death. After years of asceticism and meditation under a Bodhi tree at Bodh Gaya, he achieved enlightenment (nirvana). Core teachings: Four Noble Truths — (1) life involves suffering (dukkha), (2) suffering arises from craving/attachment, (3) suffering can end (nirvana), (4) the Eightfold Path leads to liberation (right understanding, intention, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, concentration). Three marks of existence: impermanence (anicca), suffering (dukkha), no-self (anatta — no permanent fixed self). Three main schools: Theravada (Southeast Asia, 'original' teachings), Mahayana (East Asia, bodhisattva ideal — delay enlightenment to help others), Vajrayana/Tibetan (mantra, visualization, Dalai Lama).

Stoicism is an ancient Greek philosophy (founded by Zeno of Citium ~300 BC, flourished in Rome) that has seen a major modern revival. Core idea: we cannot control external events but we can always control our response to them. The Stoic dichotomy of control: distinguish what is 'up to us' (our thoughts, judgments, desires, actions) from what is not (health, reputation, wealth, others' actions). Focus only on what's up to you. Key Stoics: Epictetus (former slave — 'We cannot choose our external circumstances but we can always choose how we respond'), Marcus Aurelius (Roman Emperor, Meditations — personal journal of Stoic practice), Seneca (letters on Stoic living). 'Memento mori' (remember you will die): Stoic practice of contemplating mortality to appreciate life and prioritize what matters. Negative visualization: imagine losing what you have to appreciate it. Modern applications: CBT therapy is largely built on Stoic principles.

Economic schools of thought: Classical economics (Adam Smith — 'The Wealth of Nations' 1776: free markets, division of labor, the 'invisible hand' whereby individual self-interest leads to collective benefit; David Ricardo — comparative advantage). Marxism (Karl Marx — 'Das Kapital' 1867: capitalism contains inherent contradictions, surplus value extracted from workers, historical materialism). Keynesian economics (John Maynard Keynes — 'The General Theory' 1936: markets are not always self-correcting; government should use fiscal policy — spending and taxation — to stabilize the economy during recessions; 'In the long run we are all dead'). Monetarism (Milton Friedman — money supply determines inflation; central banks should grow money supply steadily; free markets work better than government intervention). Behavioral economics (Thaler, Kahneman — humans are not 'rational actors'; cognitive biases systematically affect economic decisions). Modern Monetary Theory (MMT — governments that issue their own currency cannot go bankrupt; inflation, not debt, is the constraint).

The periodic table organizes 118 confirmed elements by atomic number (proton count). Periods (rows): electron shells being filled. Groups (columns): similar chemical properties. Group 1 alkali metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr): extremely reactive, soft, low melting points — explode in water. Group 2 alkaline earth metals (Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra): less reactive. Groups 3-12 transition metals: include iron (Fe), copper (Cu), gold (Au), silver (Ag), zinc (Zn), platinum (Pt) — complex electron configurations, good conductors. Group 17 halogens (F, Cl, Br, I, At): highly reactive nonmetals — F is the most reactive element. Group 18 noble gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn): completely stable (full outer electron shells), almost never react. Most abundant in Earth's crust: oxygen (46%), silicon (28%), aluminum (8%), iron (5%). Most abundant in universe: hydrogen (75%), helium (23%). Heaviest stable element: lead (Pb, 82). Elements 93-118 are all synthetic (man-made).

Ecology studies how organisms interact with each other and their environment. Food chains: producers (plants using photosynthesis) → primary consumers (herbivores) → secondary consumers (carnivores) → tertiary consumers (apex predators). Food webs are interconnected food chains. Trophic levels: energy is lost (~90%) at each level — this is why there are far more herbivores than carnivores, and why it's more energy-efficient to eat plants than meat. Keystone species: a species with disproportionate impact on its ecosystem relative to its abundance — remove it and the ecosystem collapses (sea otters eat sea urchins which eat kelp; without otters, urchins overpopulate and destroy kelp forests). Biodiversity: the variety of life — important for ecosystem resilience. The '6th mass extinction': Earth is currently experiencing the worst mass extinction since the asteroid that killed the dinosaurs, driven entirely by human activity (habitat loss, climate change, pollution, hunting). ~1 million species currently threatened with extinction.

Telescopes: optical (visible light), radio (Arecibo Observatory — collapsed 2020; now Green Bank Observatory), X-ray (Chandra), infrared, gamma-ray. Hubble Space Telescope (1990, above atmosphere — no atmospheric distortion): transformed astronomy, discovered the universe's expansion is accelerating, confirmed supermassive black holes, determined the universe's age, imaged distant galaxies (Hubble Deep Field — 3,000 galaxies in a patch of 'empty' sky). James Webb Space Telescope (JWST, launched Dec 25, 2021): 6.5m primary mirror (vs Hubble's 2.4m), observes in infrared (seeing through dust clouds and further back in time), at L2 point 1.5 million km from Earth. First images released July 2022. Can see galaxies from 13.1 billion years ago (just 700 million years after the Big Bang). Has found unexpected structures in the early universe that challenge models.

Cells are the basic units of life. Prokaryotic cells (bacteria, archaea): no nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles, usually 1-10 micrometers. Eukaryotic cells (animals, plants, fungi, protists): have a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles. Key organelles: nucleus (stores DNA, controls cell), mitochondria ('powerhouse of the cell' — produces ATP via cellular respiration; has its own DNA, evidence of ancient bacterial endosymbiosis), ribosomes (make proteins), endoplasmic reticulum (protein/lipid synthesis), Golgi apparatus (packages and ships proteins), lysosomes (cellular recycling — digest waste), cell membrane (phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins). Cell division: Mitosis (body cells) — produces two identical daughter cells (for growth and repair). Meiosis (sex cells/gametes) — produces four genetically unique cells with half the chromosomes (enables sexual reproduction and genetic diversity).

Vaccine history: Edward Jenner (1796) noticed milkmaids exposed to cowpox didn't get smallpox — inoculated 8-year-old James Phipps with cowpox then exposed him to smallpox (he didn't get it) — first vaccine. 'Vaccine' from Latin 'vacca' (cow). Louis Pasteur extended the principle — developed vaccines for chicken cholera (1880), anthrax (1881), and rabies (1885 — first human use, saved 9-year-old Joseph Meister). Smallpox: the only human disease successfully eradicated — vaccination campaign completed eradication in 1980 (WHO certified). Polio: nearly eradicated — Jonas Salk (inactivated vaccine, 1955) and Albert Sabin (oral live vaccine, 1961). Endemic polio now only in Afghanistan and Pakistan. mRNA vaccines (COVID-19): Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna were 90%+ effective in trials — faster development than any vaccine in history (10-15 years normally → less than 1 year using platform technology developed over decades).

Cybersecurity protects systems and data from attacks. Common attacks: phishing (deceptive emails/sites to steal credentials — responsible for 90% of breaches), malware (viruses, trojans, ransomware, spyware, keyloggers), SQL injection, XSS, DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service — overwhelm servers with traffic), man-in-the-middle, social engineering. Encryption: symmetric (same key to encrypt and decrypt — AES-256 for data at rest) vs asymmetric (public key to encrypt, private key to decrypt — RSA, used in HTTPS). End-to-end encryption (E2EE): only sender and recipient can read messages (Signal, WhatsApp — even the company can't access). Passwords: use a password manager (1Password, Bitwarden, LastPass — generate and store unique complex passwords per site). Enable 2FA (two-factor authentication) everywhere — even if password is stolen, attacker needs the second factor. VPN (Virtual Private Network): encrypts internet traffic and masks IP address — useful on public WiFi, but doesn't make you anonymous.

Core programming concepts: Recursion: a function calling itself — must have a base case (stopping condition) or you get infinite recursion/stack overflow. Classic examples: factorial(n) = n × factorial(n-1), Fibonacci, tree traversal. Debugging: the process of finding and fixing bugs. Approaches: print debugging (add print/console.log statements), using a debugger (set breakpoints, inspect variables step by step), rubber duck debugging (explain code to an inanimate object — often reveals the bug), reading error messages carefully, binary search (comment out half the code to find which half has the bug). API (Application Programming Interface): a defined way for software to communicate — defines what requests can be made, how to make them, and what responses look like. Library: pre-written code you import to use (e.g., lodash, numpy). Framework: a structure that dictates how you build your application (you fill in the blanks — React, Django, Rails). 'Don't reinvent the wheel' — use existing libraries.

Open source software: source code is publicly available — anyone can read, modify, and distribute it. The Free Software Foundation (Richard Stallman, 1983): GNU project to create a free Unix-like OS. GNU General Public License (GPL): 'copyleft' — any derivative must also be open source. Linus Torvalds released the Linux kernel under GPL in 1991. Linux + GNU tools = GNU/Linux. MIT License: permissive — can use in proprietary software (used by React, jQuery). Apache License: similar permissive. BSD licenses: variants, permissive. Major open source projects: Linux kernel, Android, Chromium (base for Chrome and Edge), Firefox, VS Code, Python, Node.js, React, Vue, Kubernetes, Docker, PostgreSQL, MySQL, WordPress, WordPress, TensorFlow, PyTorch. GitHub has 100 million developers and hosts most open source projects. Open source underpins essentially all of the internet. The Open Source Initiative certifies licenses.

Web3: the vision of a decentralized internet built on blockchain technology — where users own their data and digital assets (contrasted with Web2 where platforms like Facebook and Google own your data). Smart contracts: self-executing code on a blockchain — automatically enforces terms without intermediaries. Written in Solidity (Ethereum). NFTs (Non-Fungible Tokens): unique blockchain tokens representing ownership of a digital asset — art, music, game items. Peak hype in 2021 (Beeple's NFT sold for $69 million). Market has since crashed dramatically. DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations): organizations governed by token holders via smart contracts — no traditional management hierarchy. DeFi (Decentralized Finance): financial services without banks or intermediaries (lending, borrowing, trading via protocols like Uniswap, Aave, Compound). Critics: environmental impact (PoW blockchains), speculation, scams, and whether true decentralization is achieved in practice. The SEC has taken regulatory action against many crypto projects.

Virtual Reality (VR): fully immersive digital environment replacing physical reality. Headsets: Meta Quest (standalone, no PC required), PlayStation VR2, Valve Index, Apple Vision Pro (2024, $3,499, mixed reality). Applications: gaming (Beat Saber, Half-Life: Alyx), training (surgical simulation, military, pilot training), therapy (PTSD, phobias, chronic pain), virtual meetings, and architecture visualization. Motion sickness affects many users — caused by latency between head movement and display update (less than 20ms is key). Augmented Reality (AR): overlays digital content on the real world. Consumer: smartphone AR (Pokémon GO, IKEA Place), Apple/Google AR frameworks (ARKit/ARCore). Enterprise: Microsoft HoloLens (industrial/medical training). 'Metaverse': persistent, shared virtual spaces — Meta (Facebook) invested $36 billion in the concept with mixed results. The term comes from Neal Stephenson's 1992 novel 'Snow Crash.'

New York City is the most populous US city (~8.3 million city, ~20 million metro) and one of the world's great cities — a global financial, cultural, and media capital. Five boroughs: Manhattan (financial and cultural core), Brooklyn (most populous), Queens (most ethnically diverse urban area on Earth — 160+ languages spoken), The Bronx (only mainland borough, birthplace of hip hop), Staten Island. Wall Street: global financial hub, home to the NYSE and NASDAQ. Famous landmarks: Statue of Liberty (gift from France, 1886, 93m with pedestal), Central Park (341 hectares in the middle of Manhattan), Empire State Building (443m, 1931, was world's tallest for 40 years), One World Trade Center (541m — tallest in the Western Hemisphere), Times Square, Brooklyn Bridge (1883, 1,825m span). NYC has ~800 languages spoken — the most linguistically diverse city on Earth.

London is the capital of the United Kingdom and England — one of the world's most visited cities. Population: ~9.5 million city, ~14 million metro. The Thames (pronounced 'Temz') runs through the city. Key landmarks: Buckingham Palace (official royal residence), Houses of Parliament and Big Ben (the Elizabeth Tower's bell), Tower of London (1066, Crown Jewels), Tower Bridge (1894, often called 'London Bridge'), the London Eye (135m Ferris wheel), Tate Modern (art museum in former power station). The London Underground ('the Tube') is the world's oldest metro system (1863). UK GDP: ~$3.1 trillion (6th largest). The UK left the EU (Brexit) January 31, 2020. The monarchy: King Charles III became king September 2022 following Queen Elizabeth II's death after 70 years — the longest-reigning British monarch. The BBC (British Broadcasting Corporation) is the world's oldest national broadcaster (1927).

Tokyo is Japan's capital and the world's most populous metropolitan area (~37.4 million people in the greater metro area). It became Japan's capital in 1869 (before that, Kyoto was the imperial capital). Districts: Shibuya (fashion, youth culture, the famous Scramble Crossing — 3,000 people cross per green light), Shinjuku (entertainment, Golden Gai bars, Tokyo's main station — the world's busiest with 3.6 million daily passengers), Akihabara (electronics and anime), Ginza (luxury shopping), Asakusa (traditional, Senso-ji temple). Tokyo has the most Michelin-starred restaurants of any city on Earth (~300 starred restaurants). Japan's Shinkansen ('bullet train') network: up to 320 km/h, operates since 1964, carries 450,000 passengers daily, and in its history has never had a fatal accident due to derailment or collision. Mount Fuji: 3,776m, last erupted 1707, about 200,000 people climb it per year.

Italy (official: Italian Republic) has ~60 million people in 301,340 km². Capital: Rome. GDP: ~$2.2 trillion (8th largest). Italy has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country (58 — Colosseum, Pompeii, Venice and its Lagoon, Florence, Cinque Terre, Amalfi Coast, etc.). Rome: the 'Eternal City,' built on 7 hills, once capital of the Roman Empire. Major sights: Colosseum (80 AD, 50,000-80,000 spectators), Pantheon (125 AD, concrete dome with oculus), Vatican City (smallest sovereign state: 0.44 km², home to the Pope and St. Peter's Basilica). Italian contributions: the Renaissance (Florence), opera (invented in Florence ~1600), pizza, pasta, espresso, fashion (Milan — Versace, Prada, Gucci, Armani), Alfa Romeo, Ferrari, Lamborghini, Ducati. Italy is one of the world's top wine producers. The Italian language directly descended from Latin.

Spain has ~47 million people in 505,990 km². Capital: Madrid. Barcelona is the second city (capital of Catalonia — strong independence movement). GDP: ~$1.6 trillion (13th). Known for: flamenco (music, singing, and dance from Andalusia), bullfighting (divisive — support is declining), La Tomatina (tomato-throwing festival), the Running of the Bulls (Pamplona), paella (Valencian rice dish), tapas, sangria. Architecture: Antoni Gaudí's Sagrada Família in Barcelona (under construction since 1882, still not finished), Park Güell, Casa Batlló. The Spanish language: spoken by 500 million people (2nd most spoken native language globally, behind Mandarin) — 21 sovereign nations use Spanish as official language. Spain had the world's largest empire in the 16th century (stretching from the Americas to the Philippines). Spain is a constitutional monarchy (King Felipe VI).

Penguins are flightless seabirds found exclusively in the Southern Hemisphere (mostly Antarctica, but also in South America, South Africa, New Zealand, and the Galápagos Islands — they never live in the Arctic). 18 species. Their wings evolved into flippers — they 'fly' underwater at up to 36 km/h. Emperor penguin: the largest (up to 1.2m, 45 kg). Males incubate the single egg on their feet through the Antarctic winter (-40°C, 200 km/h winds) for 65-75 days without eating — they huddle together (up to 5,000 in a group) and rotate through the center. During this time they lose 40% of their body weight. Penguins are monogamous within a season and recognize their mate by voice among thousands. They have a specialized supraorbital gland to filter salt from seawater. Penguin droppings (guano) create islands of vegetation and nitrogen-rich soil in the Antarctic.

Raptors (birds of prey) have sharp talons (for gripping prey), hooked beaks (for tearing flesh), and exceptional eyesight (4-8× sharper than humans — they can spot a rabbit from 3 km away, seeing UV light that makes rodent urine trails glow). Bald eagle (USA national symbol): 2m wingspan, feeds primarily on fish (steals from other birds — they're kleptoparasites); recovered from near extinction after DDT was banned 1972. Golden eagle: fastest in level flight of large raptors. Peregrine falcon: fastest animal on Earth in a stoop (dive) at ~389 km/h — strikes prey mid-air. Harpy eagle (South America): most powerful raptor — talons the size of a grizzly bear's claws, takes sloths and monkeys. Andean condor: largest wingspan of any raptor (3.3m), soars on thermals for hours without flapping, scavenges carcasses. Owls: primarily nocturnal, rotate head 270°, asymmetric ears for triangulating prey location in total darkness.

Fish basics: Salmon are anadromous — born in freshwater streams, migrate to the ocean (where they grow for 2-7 years), then return to their exact birth stream to spawn using Earth's magnetic field and the stream's unique chemical 'smell.' Pacific salmon die after spawning (their decomposing bodies fertilize the forest). Tuna: highly migratory, warm-blooded (unlike most fish — maintain body temperature above surrounding water for better muscle performance), bluefin tuna can swim at 70 km/h and dive to 1,000m. They can be worth $1M+ at Japanese auctions. Clownfish (like Nemo): live symbiotically with sea anemones (immune to their sting). All clownfish are born male — the dominant fish in a group becomes female; if the female dies, the dominant male changes sex. Goldfish: can live 20+ years (world record 43 years) but their '3-second memory' is a myth — they can remember things for months. Fish have been on Earth for 530 million years.

Parrots (order Psittaciformes, ~398 species) are among the most intelligent birds — some have demonstrated language comprehension beyond simple mimicry. Alex the African grey parrot (Dr. Irene Pepperberg's research, 30 years): could identify ~100 objects, request items, answer questions about shape/color/material, and understood concepts like 'same,' 'different,' 'bigger,' 'smaller,' and 'zero.' He said 'You be good, I love you' on his last night alive (2007). African grey parrots are considered the most intelligent parrots. Cockatoos can solve multi-step lock puzzles — demonstrating causal reasoning. Kea (New Zealand alpine parrot): the only alpine parrot, extremely curious and tool-using. Parrots can live 50-90 years — some outlive their owners. They need mental stimulation; boredom leads to feather-plucking. ~1/3 of parrot species are threatened due to habitat loss and the pet trade.

Michael Phelps is the most decorated Olympian in history: 28 Olympic medals (23 gold, 3 silver, 2 bronze) across 4 Olympics (2000-2016).
