He has a 193cm wingspan (taller than his 193cm height — unusual), double-jointed ankles that flex 15° further than average (giving flipper-like propulsion), and produces less lactic acid than average swimmers. Swimming strokes (in order of speed): butterfly (fastest, most demanding), backstroke, breaststroke (slowest), freestyle/front crawl. Competition pool: 50m (long course) or 25m (short course). World record 100m freestyle: ~46.8 seconds (~7.7 km/h). Open water swimming: 10km Olympic event, plus marathon swims across channels. The English Channel (34 km): Matthew Webb first swam it in 1875 (21 hours 45 minutes). Modern record: ~6.5 hours.

Formula 1 is the highest class of single-seater auto racing, governed by the FIA. A season: 20-24 Grands Prix at circuits worldwide. F1 cars reach 350+ km/h, generate 3.5G in cornering (more than an astronaut at launch), brake from 300 to 100 km/h in 2.7 seconds. The car is largely aerodynamic — about 70% of performance is the car, 30% the driver. Constructors: Ferrari (most successful historically), Mercedes (dominant 2014-2021 — won 8 consecutive constructors' championships), Red Bull (dominant 2022-2023 — Max Verstappen won consecutive world championships). Drivers' world champions: Michael Schumacher (7 titles, 2001-2004 dominance), Lewis Hamilton (7 titles — tied with Schumacher, first Black Formula 1 champion), Ayrton Senna (3, died Imola 1994 — still considered by many the greatest ever), Max Verstappen (3+, currently dominant). Pit stop record: ~1.82 seconds (Red Bull, 2023).

Martial arts span thousands of years. Japanese: Judo (Jigoro Kano, 1882 — throws and grappling, Olympic since 1964), Karate (Okinawan, strikes and blocks), Aikido (circular throws using attacker's momentum), Jujutsu (parent art of judo and BJJ). Korean: Taekwondo (high kicks, Olympic since 2000), Hapkido. Chinese: Kung Fu/Wushu (umbrella term for many styles — Wing Chun, Shaolin, Tai Chi), Tai Chi (meditative, slow movements, internal energy). Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu (BJJ): developed by the Gracie family from Japanese judo/jujutsu — focuses on ground fighting and submissions. Demonstrated that a smaller person can defeat a larger one using leverage and technique. Boxing: 'the sweet science' — 8 weight classes from strawweight (47.6 kg) to heavyweight (90+ kg). Muhammad Ali (1942-2016): widely considered the greatest — 56-5 record, 'float like a butterfly, sting like a bee.' MMA (Mixed Martial Arts): UFC (founded 1993) combines striking and grappling disciplines.

Electronic music evolved from early synthesizers and tape manipulation. Key developments: Moog synthesizer (Robert Moog, 1964 — enabled affordable electronic music), Kraftwerk (German pioneers of electronic music, 1970s — influenced virtually all electronic music since), Roland TR-808 drum machine (1980, its bass drum sound is in thousands of songs — distinctive boom), Roland TB-303 bassline synthesizer (1981 — accidental discovery of the acid house sound). Genres: Disco (1970s New York — 4/4 kick drum, orchestral, gay club scene), House (Chicago, early 1980s — Frankie Knuckles at The Warehouse), Techno (Detroit, mid-1980s — Juan Atkins, Derrick May, Kevin Saunderson — 'The Belleville Three'), Trance (Germany, early 1990s — emotional, melodic), Drum and Bass/Jungle (UK, 1990s — fast breakbeats ~174 BPM), Dubstep (South London, early 2000s — Skream, Benga, Digital Mystikz — evolved into EDM 'brostep' via Skrillex). Deadmau5, Daft Punk, Avicii, Calvin Harris, David Guetta defined mainstream EDM.

The Beatles (John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, Ringo Starr) are the best-selling music artists in history — 600 million+ records sold. From Liverpool, formed 1960. Manager Brian Epstein, producer George Martin ('Fifth Beatle'). US arrival Feb 9, 1964 (Ed Sullivan Show — 73 million viewers, 45% of US population) launched Beatlemania and the British Invasion. Evolution: early Merseybeat pop ('Please Please Me,' 'She Loves You') → sophisticated rock ('Rubber Soul,' 1965) → psychedelia ('Revolver,' 1966; 'Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band,' 1967 — first concept album?) → 'White Album' (1968) → 'Abbey Road' (1969) → Let It Be (1970, breakup). Lennon-McCartney: greatest songwriting partnership in pop history. Lennon assassinated Dec 8, 1980 in New York. McCartney still tours at 80+. The Rolling Stones: formed 1962, 'World's Greatest Rock and Roll Band,' still touring — Mick Jagger, Keith Richards.

Globalization: the increasing interconnection of the world's economies, cultures, and populations through trade, communication, and migration. The WTO (World Trade Organization, founded 1995, 164 members): sets global trade rules and resolves disputes. Free trade agreements (FTAs): reduce tariffs and barriers between member countries — NAFTA/USMCA (USA/Canada/Mexico), EU single market, TPP/CPTPP. Comparative advantage (Ricardo, 1817): countries benefit from specializing in what they produce most efficiently and trading — even if one country is better at everything. Global supply chains: Apple designs in California, sources components from 43 countries, manufactures in China, sells globally. COVID-19 revealed supply chain vulnerabilities — computer chip shortage shut down car production. Deglobalization trends: geopolitical tensions, reshoring ('friend-shoring'), and supply chain diversification. China's Belt and Road Initiative: infrastructure lending and development across 150+ countries — criticized as 'debt trap diplomacy.'

World population: 8 billion reached November 15, 2022. Growth: from 1 billion (1804) → 2B (1927) → 3B (1960) → 4B (1974) → 5B (1987) → 6B (1999) → 7B (2011) → 8B (2022). Projected ~10 billion by 2050-2100 then stabilization/decline. Demographic transition: as countries develop, birth rates fall after death rates — high birth/death → high birth/low death (population boom) → low birth/low death (stable). Fertility rate of 2.1 children per woman maintains stable population. Most of Europe, East Asia, and Latin America are below replacement fertility. Japan, South Korea, Italy, Germany are shrinking. Africa and parts of South Asia still growing. Aging: by 2050, 2 billion people will be over 65 (vs 700 million today). Migration: ~281 million international migrants worldwide (3.6% of world population). Climate change projected to drive 200 million+ climate refugees by 2050.

Global inequality: the world's 2,668 billionaires have as much wealth as the bottom 60% of humanity combined (Oxfam). Gini coefficient: measures income inequality (0 = perfect equality, 1 = one person has everything). Most equal: Nordic countries (Denmark, Norway, Sweden, Finland — Gini ~0.25-0.28). Most unequal: South Africa (0.63), Brazil, Colombia, Namibia, Zambia. US Gini: ~0.41 (high for a wealthy nation). Extreme poverty: World Bank defines as living under $2.15/day (2022 dollars) — dropped from 36% of world population (1990) to ~9% (2022) — massive progress. About 700 million people still live in extreme poverty. Human Rights: the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UN, 1948, Eleanor Roosevelt was instrumental): 30 articles establishing civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights. The UN Human Rights Council monitors violations. NGOs: Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch document violations globally.

Mass media evolution: printing press (Gutenberg, ~1440) → newspapers (17th century) → radio (1920s) → television (1950s) → internet (1990s) → social media (2000s) → algorithms (2010s+). News media: broadsheets (NYT, Washington Post, Guardian, Le Monde), tabloids, broadcast news (BBC, CNN, Fox News), public radio (NPR, BBC Radio). Misinformation/disinformation: false information spreading faster than corrections on social media — MIT study (2018): false news spread 6× faster than true news on Twitter. Algorithms: optimize for engagement (outrage, fear, and tribalism drive more clicks) — reinforcing filter bubbles and polarization. 'Deep fakes': AI-generated fake videos of real people — increasingly convincing and dangerous. Media literacy: critical skills to evaluate source credibility, identify bias, and check information across multiple sources. Journalistic ethics: independence, accuracy, fairness, accountability, minimizing harm. Newspapers' business model is in crisis (ad revenue → Google/Facebook); many local papers have closed.

There are ~7,100 languages spoken worldwide — half may be extinct by 2100. Most spoken by native speakers: Mandarin Chinese (~920 million), Spanish (~475 million), English (~370 million), Hindi (~340 million), Arabic (~315 million), Bengali, Portuguese, Russian, Japanese, Yue Chinese (Cantonese). Most spoken including second-language speakers: English (1.5 billion total). Languages die at ~2 per week. The Linguistic Society of America (1924) and UNESCO work on language documentation. Language families: Indo-European (English, Spanish, Hindi, Russian, Persian — descended from a common ancestor ~6,000 years ago), Sino-Tibetan (Mandarin, Tibetan, Burmese), Afro-Asiatic (Arabic, Hebrew, Amharic), Niger-Congo (Swahili, Yoruba, Zulu — 1,500+ languages), Austronesian (Malay, Tagalog, Hawaiian, Māori). Sign languages are full natural languages — ASL (American Sign Language) has its own grammar different from English.

English has borrowed extensively from Latin and Ancient Greek — understanding roots unlocks thousands of words. Latin roots: aqua (water: aquatic, aquarium), bene (good: benefit, benevolent), bio (life: biology, biography), cent (hundred: century, percent), dict (say: dictate, dictionary, predict), duc/duce (lead: conduct, deduce, education), fac/fact (make/do: factory, manufacture, artifact), juris (law: jury, justice, jurisdiction), locus (place: location, local), mal (bad: malfunction, malevolent), man/manu (hand: manual, manufacture), mort (death: mortal, immortal), port (carry: transport, portable, export), scrib/script (write: describe, manuscript, inscription), spec/spect (look: spectacle, inspect), terra (earth: territory, terrain), vid/vis (see: video, visible, vision), vit/viv (live: vital, vivid). Greek roots: chrono (time), demos (people), geo (earth), logos (word/study), micro/macro, mono/poly, ology (study of), phone (sound), photo (light), tele (far), thermo (heat).

Money's three functions: medium of exchange (avoids barter), store of value (save purchasing power), and unit of account (measure value). Types: commodity money (gold, silver — intrinsic value), representative money (backed by a commodity), fiat money (value by government decree — all modern currencies). The US dollar: world's reserve currency since Bretton Woods Agreement (1944) — most global trade is priced in dollars. Central banks: set interest rates (higher rates → more expensive to borrow → less spending/investment → reduces inflation; lower rates → opposite). The Federal Reserve (US): 'dual mandate' — stable prices AND maximum employment. ECB (Europe). Inflation: too much money chasing too few goods. Hyperinflation: Zimbabwe (2008 — 79.6 billion percent monthly inflation rate, prices doubling every 24 hours), Weimar Germany (1923), Venezuela (2018+). Deflation: falling prices sound good but cause economic death spirals (consumers wait to buy, businesses can't invest). Central banks target ~2% inflation as ideal.

Silicon Valley (San Francisco Bay Area) has been the world's innovation hub since the semiconductor industry emerged in the 1950s-60s (Fairchild Semiconductor, Intel). Why there: Stanford and Berkeley universities, venture capital ecosystem, risk-accepting culture, dense network of founders and talent. Venture capital (VC): investors provide capital to early-stage companies in exchange for equity — accepting high risk for potential outsized returns. Stages: pre-seed/angel → seed → Series A → B → C → D... IPO (Initial Public Offering): first sale of shares to the public on a stock exchange. Unicorn: private startup valued at $1 billion+ (coined by Aileen Lee, 2013). Decacorn: $10B+. Hectocorn: $100B+. Key VC firms: Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz (a16z), Benchmark, Accel, Kleiner Perkins. The Lean Startup (Eric Ries): build-measure-learn feedback loop, minimum viable product (MVP). Most startups fail: ~90% within 10 years. 'Fail fast' philosophy.

Space exploration milestones: Sputnik (USSR, Oct 4, 1957 — first satellite), Laika (first animal in space, 1957), Yuri Gagarin (first human in space, April 12, 1961, 108 minutes), John Glenn (first American to orbit Earth, 1962), Apollo 11 Moon landing (July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin — 'one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind'), Apollo 13 (1970, 'Houston we have a problem,' remarkable rescue mission). Space Shuttle program (1981-2011): 135 missions, 2 disasters (Challenger 1986, Columbia 2003). ISS (International Space Station): continuously inhabited since November 2000, 16-nation collaboration. New space race: SpaceX (reusable rockets, Starship), Blue Origin (Jeff Bezos, New Shepard and New Glenn), NASA Artemis program (return humans to Moon, aim for 2025-2026), Chinese space program (Tiangong space station, lunar ambitions), commercial lunar economy. Voyager 1 (launched 1977): now 24+ billion km from Earth — the farthest human-made object.

Photography fundamentals — the 'exposure triangle': Aperture (f-stop): the opening in the lens. Wider aperture (smaller f-number: f/1.4, f/2) = more light, shallower depth of field (blurry background — 'bokeh'). Narrower aperture (f/11, f/16) = less light, more of the scene in focus. Shutter speed: duration the sensor is exposed. Faster (1/1000s): freezes motion. Slower (1/30s, 1s+): blurs motion, requires tripod. ISO: sensor sensitivity. Lower ISO (100, 200): less light absorbed, cleaner image. Higher ISO (3200, 6400+): more sensitive but adds 'noise' (grain). Composition rules (guidelines, not laws): Rule of thirds (divide frame into 3×3 grid, place subjects at intersections), leading lines (guide viewer's eye), framing (use foreground elements), symmetry, negative space, and 'if in doubt, zoom out.' RAW vs JPEG: RAW files contain all sensor data (much larger, requires editing software but far more flexibility) vs JPEG (compressed, ready to share).

Architecture history: Ancient: Pyramids of Giza (~2560 BC), Parthenon (Athens, 447 BC), Roman Colosseum (80 AD), Pantheon (125 AD — unreinforced concrete dome still standing). Medieval: Gothic cathedrals — flying buttresses transferred weight outward enabling tall thin walls with huge stained glass windows (Notre-Dame de Paris, Chartres, Cologne Cathedral). Renaissance: symmetry, proportion, classical references (Brunelleschi's Florence Cathedral dome — the first large dome since Antiquity). Baroque: dramatic, ornate, theatrical (St. Peter's Basilica, Versailles). Modernism (20th century): 'form follows function' (Louis Sullivan), glass and steel, rejection of ornamentation — Bauhaus school (Gropius), Le Corbusier (brutalism), Mies van der Rohe (minimalism). Frank Lloyd Wright: organic architecture, Fallingwater (cantilevered over a waterfall), Guggenheim Museum. Tallest buildings: Burj Khalifa, Dubai (828m, 2010); Merdeka 118, Malaysia (679m, 2023); Shanghai Tower (632m).

Plastic pollution: ~400 million tonnes of plastic produced annually; ~14 million tonnes enter the ocean per year. The Great Pacific Garbage Patch: a gyre of marine debris twice the size of Texas (~1.6 million km²), mostly microplastics (not a solid island — dispersed particles). Microplastics (<5mm): breakdown of larger plastics; also from synthetic clothing washing (microfibers), tire wear, and industrial processes. Found in: Arctic snow, deep ocean trenches (Mariana Trench), Arctic sea ice, human blood (2022 study), breast milk, placentas, and the air. Health effects: under active research. Only ~9% of all plastic ever produced has been recycled; 12% was incinerated; 79% is in landfills or the environment. Solutions: reduce single-use plastics, improve waste management in developing countries (most ocean plastic comes from Asia — especially rivers), extended producer responsibility, chemical recycling. Plastic takes 450+ years to decompose.

Human evolution: Hominins (bipedal apes) diverged from chimpanzees ~6-7 million years ago. Key ancestors: Australopithecus (3.9-2.9 Ma, Africa — Lucy skeleton discovered 1974, 3.2 million years old), Homo habilis ('handy man,' ~2.4-1.4 Ma — first stone tools), Homo erectus (1.9 Ma-140,000 years ago — first hominin to leave Africa, mastered fire), Homo heidelbergensis, Homo neanderthalensis (Neanderthals, 400,000-40,000 years ago — Europe and Asia, large brains, used tools, buried dead, made art; Homo sapiens interbred with them — non-Africans are ~2% Neanderthal). Homo sapiens: anatomically modern humans evolved in Africa ~300,000 years ago. Out of Africa migration: ~60,000-70,000 years ago — expanded across the world, reaching Australia ~65,000 years ago, Americas ~15,000+ years ago. The 'Great Leap Forward' ~50,000 years ago: sudden behavioral modernity — complex art, music, ritual, and symbolic thinking (cave art in Chauvet Cave, 36,000 years old).

AI history: Alan Turing (1912-1954): broke the Enigma code in WWII, proposed the 'Turing Test' (1950 — if a machine's conversation is indistinguishable from a human's, it can be considered 'intelligent'), invented theoretical foundations of computing. 'The Imitation Game' (2014 film). Dartmouth Conference (1956): John McCarthy coined 'artificial intelligence.' Early AI: symbolic AI ('good old-fashioned AI') — explicit rules and logic. AI winters (1974-80, 1987-93): hype exceeded progress, funding dried up. Expert systems (1980s): knowledge bases. Machine learning (1980s-90s): algorithms learned from data. Deep Blue (IBM, 1997): defeated world chess champion Garry Kasparov — milestone in narrow AI. ImageNet and deep learning breakthrough (2012, AlexNet): convolutional neural networks dramatically outperformed other methods in image recognition — sparked the modern deep learning era. AlphaGo (DeepMind, 2016): defeated Lee Sedol 4-1 at Go (far harder than chess — 10^170 possible positions). GPT-3 (OpenAI, 2020) and ChatGPT (2022): large language models surprised the world with general conversational ability.

Legal systems worldwide: Common law (UK, USA, Canada, Australia): judge-made case law ('precedent') shapes the law — courts are bound by past decisions (stare decisis). Civil law (France, Germany, Japan, most of Europe and Latin America): comprehensive legal codes, less emphasis on precedent. Sharia law: Islamic law from Quran and Hadith — used in varying degrees in Muslim-majority countries (Saudi Arabia, Iran: strictly; others: partially or in family law only). Constitutional law: a constitution is the supreme law — all other laws must comply. US Bill of Rights (First 10 Amendments): freedom of speech/religion/press, right to bear arms, protection from unreasonable searches, right to jury trial, protection against self-incrimination, due process, right to an attorney. International law: treaties (binding agreements between states), customary international law, ICC (International Criminal Court — prosecutes war crimes, crimes against humanity, genocide). Habeas corpus: the right not to be imprisoned without legal justification — a cornerstone of civil liberty.

The ancient Olympics began in 776 BC at Olympia, Greece — held every 4 years (Olympiad) in honor of Zeus. Athletes competed nude, only Greek men allowed (though Spartan women competed in a separate festival). Events: foot races (stadion = ~192m — origin of 'stadium'), wrestling, discus, javelin, chariot racing. Theodosius I abolished them in 393 AD as pagan. Pierre de Coubertin (French educator) revived the Olympics — first modern games in Athens, 1896: 14 nations, 241 athletes, 43 events. Now: 200+ nations, 10,000+ athletes, 300+ events, alternating Summer (every 4 years) and Winter Games (also every 4 years, offset by 2). 2024 Summer: Paris. The Paralympic Games (for athletes with physical disabilities) was formalized in 1960, now held alongside every Olympics. World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) tests athletes. Most decorated Olympian: Michael Phelps (28 medals). Most gold: Michael Phelps (23). Most Olympic team medals: USA (Summer), Norway (Winter).

Renewable energy sources: Solar photovoltaic (PV): silicon cells convert sunlight directly to electricity. Cost has fallen 90% since 2010 — now the cheapest electricity in history in most places. Global solar capacity: 1+ terawatt. Wind turbines: convert kinetic energy of wind. Offshore wind is more expensive to build but wind is stronger and more consistent. Denmark gets 55%+ of its electricity from wind. Hydroelectric: dams use falling water to generate electricity — largest source of renewable electricity (16% of global total). Three Gorges Dam (China): world's largest power station (22.5 GW). Geothermal: heat from Earth's interior — Iceland gets 66% of its energy from geothermal. Batteries/storage: key challenge — renewables are intermittent (sun doesn't always shine, wind doesn't always blow). Lithium-ion batteries (Tesla Megapack, utility-scale), pumped hydro, and green hydrogen are key storage solutions. Global clean energy investment exceeded $1 trillion for the first time in 2022. Coal use is declining; natural gas is the transition fuel. Nuclear debate continues — low carbon but expensive and slow to build.

Greatest scientists in history: Isaac Newton (1643-1727): laws of motion and universal gravitation, calculus (simultaneously with Leibniz), optics. 'If I have seen further it is by standing on the shoulders of giants.' Charles Darwin (1809-1882): theory of evolution by natural selection ('On the Origin of Species,' 1859) — the most important idea in biology. Marie Curie (1867-1934): discovered polonium and radium, first woman to win a Nobel Prize, only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences (Physics 1903, Chemistry 1911). She died from aplastic anemia caused by radiation exposure. Nikola Tesla (1856-1943): AC electrical system (vs Edison's DC), induction motor, Tesla coil. Albert Einstein (1879-1955): Special and General Relativity, photoelectric effect (Nobel 1921), E=mc². Richard Feynman (1918-1988): quantum electrodynamics (Nobel 1965), Feynman diagrams, brilliant teacher. Rosalind Franklin: X-ray crystallography crucial to discovering DNA structure (her contribution was underacknowledged — Watson, Crick, and Wilkins got the Nobel).
